en.extract:
The Mentawai malkoha (Phaenicophaeus oeneicaudus) is a species of cuckoo in the family Cuculidae. It is endemic to the Mentawai Islands off the west of Sumatra. It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the chestnut-breasted malkoha.


Taxonomy
The Mentawai malkoha was formally described in 1855 by the French naturalists Jules and Édouard Verreaux under the current binomial name Phaenicophaeus oeneicaudus. They believed that their specimen had come from Ceylon (now Sri Lanka). The Italian zoologist Tommaso Salvadori realised that this was an error and the specimen must instead have come from the Mentawai Islands which lie in the  Indian Ocean approximately 150 km (93 mi) off the western coast of Sumatra. The genus name Phaenicophaeus is from Ancient Greek φοινικοφαης (phoinikophaēs) meaning "of crimson appearance" or "red-gleaming". The specific epithet oeneicaudus combines the Latin oenoo- meaning "wine" and cauda meaning "tail". The Mentawai malkoha was formerly treated as a subspecies of the chestnut-breasted malkoha (Phaenicophaeus curvirostris). It was promoted to species status based on the morphological differences.

The '''Mentawai malkoha''' ('''''Phaenicophaeus oeneicaudus''''') is a species of [[cuckoo]] in the family [[Cuculidae]]. It is endemic to the [[Mentawai Islands Regency|Mentawai Islands]] off the west of [[Sumatra]]. It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[chestnut-breasted malkoha]].

==Taxonomy==
The Mentawai malkoha was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1855 by the French naturalists [[Jules Verreaux|Jules]] and [[Édouard Verreaux]] under the current [[binomial name]] ''Phaenicophaeus oeneicaudus''. They believed that their specimen had come from Ceylon (now [[Sri Lanka]]).<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Verreaux | first1=J. | author1-link=Jules Verreaux | last2=Verreaux | first2=E. | author2-link=Édouard Verreaux | year=1855 | title=Notice sur le genre ''Phaenicophaeus'', Vieill., ''Malcoha'', Cuv. | language=French | journal=Revue et Magasin de Zoologie Pure et Appliquée | series=2nd series | volume=7 | pages=356–357 [357] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2281220 }}</ref> The Italian zoologist [[Tommaso Salvadori]] realised that this was an error and the specimen must instead have come from the [[Mentawai Islands Regency|Mentawai Islands]] which lie in the  [[Indian Ocean]] approximately {{cvt|150|km}} off the western coast of [[Sumatra]].<ref name=salvadori>{{ cite journal | last=Salvadori | first=Tommaso  | author-link=Tommaso Salvadori | year=1894 | title=Viaggio del Dr. Elio Modigliani nelle isole Mentawei: Catalogo di una collezione di uccelli di Sipora | language=Italian, Latin | journal=Annali del Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Genova | series=2nd series | volume=14 | pages=588-601 [590-591] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7940630 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=55 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476526 }}</ref> The genus name ''Phaenicophaeus'' is from [[Ancient Greek]] φοινικοφαης (''phoinikophaēs'') meaning "of crimson appearance" or "red-gleaming".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Phaenicophaeus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Phaenicophaeus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=31 August 2024 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''oeneicaudus'' combines the [[Latin]] ''oenoo-'' meaning "wine" and ''cauda'' meaning "tail".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=oeneicaudus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=oeneicaudus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=30 August 2024 }}</ref> The Mentawai malkoha was formerly treated as a [[subspecies]] of the [[chestnut-breasted malkoha]] (''Phaenicophaeus curvirostris''). It was promoted to species status based on the morphological differences.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Turacos, bustards, cuckoos, mesites, sandgrouse | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/turacos/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=31 August 2024}}</ref>
